  line = sprintf("%-20s %8.2f %s", $1, $2, $3)
  print line
}' data.txt

items.title: Case Conversion
items.text:
# Uppercase a field
awk '{ $2 = toupper($2); print }' file.txt

# Lowercase everything
awk '{ print tolower($0) }' file.txt

# Title case (capitalize first letter of each word — gawk gensub)
awk '{
  print gensub(/\b(.)/, "\\u\\1", "g")   # \u = uppercase next char (gawk extension)
}' file.txt

items.title: String Concatenation
items.text:
awk concatenates strings by simple juxtaposition — no operator needed:

awk '{ result = $1 "-" $2 "-" $3; print result }' file.txt

awk '{ print "Hello, " $1 "!" }' names.txt

# Build a comma-separated list
awk '{
  line = ""
  for (i=1; i<=NF; i++) {
    line = line (i>1 ? "," : "") $i
  }
  print line
}' file.txt

items.title: Trim Whitespace
items.text:
awk has no built-in trim function:

# Trim leading and trailing whitespace from $0
awk '{ gsub(/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$/, ""); print }' file.txt

# Trim a specific field
awk '{ gsub(/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$/, "", $2); print }' file.txt

# Define a trim function (see user functions page)
awk '
function trim(s) {
  gsub(/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$/, "", s)
  return s
}
{ print trim($0) }
' file.txt

items.title: awk — Built-in Variables
items.text: awk's built-in variables control how input is split, how output is joined, and expose metadata about the current state of processing.

items.title: Complete Reference
items.text: Variable Default Purpose FS " " Input field separator. " " (space) = split on runs of whitespace (special). Any other value splits literally. OFS " " Output field separator. Used when printing multiple args with print $1, $2 or when awk reconstructs $0 after field assignment. RS "\n" Input record separator. Set to "" for paragraph mode. gawk: can be a regex. ORS "\n" Output record separator. Appended after each print statement. NR — Total number of records read so far (cumulative across files). NF — Number of fields in the current record. FNR — Record number within the current file only (resets to 1 at each new file). FILENAME — Name of the current input file. ARGC — Number of command-line arguments (including awk itself). ARGV — Array of command-line arguments: ARGV[0] = "awk". ENVIRON — Array of environment variables: ENVIRON["HOME"]. gawk. OFMT "%.6g" Format used when converting numbers to strings for output. CONVFMT "%.6g" Format used when converting numbers to strings internally. SUBSEP "\034" Separator for multi-dimensional array subscripts (used in arr[a,b]). FIELDWIDTHS — gawk: space-separated field widths for fixed-width input. FPAT — gawk: regex describing what a field looks like (vs what separates fields). IGNORECASE 0 gawk: set to 1 for case-insensitive regex and string comparisons. PROCINFO — gawk: array with process info (PROCINFO["pid"], PROCINFO["version"], etc.). RT — gawk: the text that matched RS (the actual record terminator).

items.title: FS and OFS
items.text:
# Set both FS and OFS in BEGIN
awk 'BEGIN{FS=":"; OFS="\t"} {print $1, $3}' /etc/passwd

# Changing OFS affects how $0 is reconstructed
awk 'BEGIN{FS=","; OFS="|"} {$1=$1; print}' data.csv
# $1=$1 forces awk to rebuild $0 with the new OFS

# Multi-character FS
awk -F' :: ' '{print $1}' structured.log

# Regex FS
awk -F'[,;|]+' '{print NF, $0}' mixed.txt

items.title: RS and Paragraph Mode
items.text:
# Paragraph mode: treat blank lines as record separators
# Each "record" is a paragraph; fields within are newline-separated
awk 'BEGIN{RS=""; FS="\n"} { print NR": first line is "$1 }' doc.txt

# Custom record separator
awk 'BEGIN{RS="---\n"} { print NR, NF }' frontmatter.md

# gawk: regex RS
awk 'BEGIN{RS="[0-9]+ "} { print NR": "$0 }' file.txt

# ORS: change line ending in output
awk 'BEGIN{ORS=","} {print $1}' file.txt | sed 's/,$/\n/'
# Prints all $1 values on one line separated by commas

items.title: ENVIRON
items.text:
# Access environment variables
awk 'BEGIN {
  print ENVIRON["HOME"]
  print ENVIRON["PATH"]
  if ("DEBUG" in ENVIRON) print "debug mode on"
}'

# Pass a shell variable into awk via -v (preferred over ENVIRON for single values)
awk -v threshold="$THRESHOLD" '$3 > threshold' data.txt

items.title: FPAT: Field-by-pattern (proper CSV)
items.text:
Standard FS="," breaks on commas inside quoted fields. FPAT describes what a field looks like instead.

# Handle CSV with quoted fields containing commas
awk 'BEGIN {
  FPAT = "([^,]*)|(\"[^\"]*\")"
} {
  print $2   # second field, even if it contains commas
}' data.csv

items.title: FIELDWIDTHS: Fixed-width input
items.text:
# Fixed-width fields: col 1-10, col 11-18, col 19-30
awk 'BEGIN{FIELDWIDTHS="10 8 12"} {print $1, $3}' fixed.txt

items.title: IGNORECASE
items.text:
# Case-insensitive matching (gawk)
awk 'BEGIN{IGNORECASE=1} /error/ {print}' logfile

# Case-insensitive comparison
awk 'BEGIN{IGNORECASE=1} $1 == "error" {print}' logfile

items.title: Setting Variables with -v
items.text:
# Pass variables from the shell
awk -v min=100 -v max=500 '$3 >= min && $3 <= max' data.txt

# Multiple -v flags
awk -v OFS='\t' -v threshold=50 '$2 > threshold {print $1, $2}' data.txt

# -v evaluated before BEGIN
awk -v x=5 'BEGIN{print x+1}'

When to use -v vs ENVIRON

Use -v var=val for single values you're passing in. Use ENVIRON when you want access to the full environment, or when the value may be a complex string (the -v flag interprets escape sequences, which can corrupt binary data or paths with backslashes).

items.title: fzf — Fuzzy Finder
items.text:
fzf is a general-purpose command-line fuzzy finder. It reads a list from stdin, displays an interactive full-screen selector UI, and writes the selection to stdout.

It's written in Go, has zero runtime dependencies, and is fast enough to filter millions of lines interactively.

items.title: How fzf Works
items.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                              │
│  stdin (any list)  →  fzf UI  →  stdout (selection)         │
│                                                              │
│  1. Read all lines from stdin                                │
│  2. Display interactive filter UI                            │
│  3. User types to narrow the list                            │
│  4. User presses Enter (or a bound key)                      │
│  5. Selected line(s) written to stdout                       │
│                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

fzf is not limited to files. Any list works: git branches, process IDs, docker containers, kubectl resources, history entries, or lines from any command.

items.title: Installation
items.text:
# macOS
brew install fzf

# Fedora / RHEL
sudo dnf install fzf

# Debian / Ubuntu
sudo apt install fzf

# From source (any platform)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/junegunn/fzf.git ~/.fzf
~/.fzf/install

items.title: Shell Integration
items.text:
Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:

eval "$(fzf --bash)"   # bash
eval "$(fzf --zsh)"    # zsh
source /usr/share/fzf/shell/key-bindings.fish   # fish

This installs Ctrl-T, Ctrl-R, and Alt-C bindings plus ** tab completion.

items.title: Basic Usage
items.text:
# Filter a list of files (uses find by default)
fzf

# Pipe anything to fzf
ls | fzf
cat /etc/passwd | cut -d: -f1 | fzf
git branch | fzf

# Capture selection into a variable
selected=$(ls | fzf)
echo "You selected: $selected"

# Use selection directly
vim $(fzf)
cd $(find . -type d | fzf)

# Multi-select (Tab to toggle)
vim $(fzf -m)

items.title: Sections
items.text: Page Contents Search Syntax Fuzzy, exact, prefix, suffix, negation, AND/OR Key Options --height, --layout, --multi, --nth, --header, and more Preview Window Real-time preview of each selection Key Bindings Default keys and custom --bind actions Shell Integration Ctrl-T, Ctrl-R, Alt-C, ** completion Environment & Config FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS, FZF_DEFAULT_COMMAND, and more Recipes Shell functions for git, docker, kubectl, and more Integrations vim/neovim, git, kubectl, package managers

items.title: fzf — Environment & Configuration
items.text: All fzf behavior can be configured through environment variables, making it easy to apply consistent defaults across all invocations.

items.title: Environment Variables
items.text: Variable Purpose FZF_DEFAULT_COMMAND Command to generate the initial list when no input is piped and no file argument given FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS Default options applied to every fzf invocation FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS_FILE Path to a file containing default options (one option per line or space-separated) FZF_CTRL_T_COMMAND Command for Ctrl-T file selection FZF_CTRL_T_OPTS Extra options for the Ctrl-T binding FZF_CTRL_R_OPTS Extra options for the Ctrl-R history search FZF_ALT_C_COMMAND Command for Alt-C directory selection FZF_ALT_C_OPTS Extra options for the Alt-C binding FZF_COMPLETION_TRIGGER Trigger string for fuzzy completion (default: **) FZF_COMPLETION_OPTS Extra options for ** completion FZF_TMUX Set to 1 to use fzf-tmux wrapper by default FZF_TMUX_OPTS Options passed to fzf-tmux

items.title: Recommended Configuration
items.text:
Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:

# ── fzf default command ─────────────────────────────────────
# Use fd: respects .gitignore, shows hidden files, fast
export FZF_DEFAULT_COMMAND='fd --type f --hidden --follow --exclude .git'

# ── fzf default options ──────────────────────────────────────
export FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS="
  --height=40%
  --layout=reverse
  --border=rounded
  --info=inline
  --bind 'ctrl-/:toggle-preview'
  --bind 'ctrl-a:select-all'
  --bind 'ctrl-d:deselect-all'
  --bind 'ctrl-f:preview-page-down'
  --bind 'ctrl-b:preview-page-up'
  --color=bg+:#1e232e,bg:#0c0e13,spinner:#9b7de8,hl:#5ac8a0
  --color=fg:#c9d1e0,header:#5ac8a0,info:#f0a05a,pointer:#9b7de8
  --color=marker:#5ac8a0,fg+:#e8edf5,prompt:#9b7de8,hl+:#5ac8a0
  --color=border:#252b38
"

# ── Ctrl-T: file finder ──────────────────────────────────────
export FZF_CTRL_T_COMMAND="$FZF_DEFAULT_COMMAND"
export FZF_CTRL_T_OPTS="
  --preview 'bat --color=always --line-range=:100 {}'
  --preview-window=right:50%
  --bind 'ctrl-/:toggle-preview'"

# ── Ctrl-R: history search ───────────────────────────────────
export FZF_CTRL_R_OPTS="
  --preview 'echo {}'
  --preview-window=down:3:hidden:wrap
  --bind 'ctrl-/:toggle-preview'
  --bind 'ctrl-y:execute-silent(echo -n {2..} | pbcopy)+abort'
  --color=header:italic
  --header='Ctrl-Y: copy command'"

# ── Alt-C: directory jump ────────────────────────────────────
export FZF_ALT_C_COMMAND='fd --type d --hidden --follow --exclude .git'
export FZF_ALT_C_OPTS="
  --preview 'tree -C {} | head -30'
  --preview-window=right:50%"

items.title: Options File
items.text:
For large option sets, use a file:

export FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS_FILE="$HOME/.fzfrc"

~/.fzfrc: 

--height=40%
--layout=reverse
--border=rounded
--color=bg+:#1e232e,fg:#c9d1e0
--bind ctrl-/:toggle-preview
--bind ctrl-a:select-all

Options in FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS_FILE are merged with FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS. Command-line options override both.

items.title: Per-command Overrides
items.text:
FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS applies everywhere, but you can override for specific invocations:

# Override height for this specific call
FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS="" fzf --height=90%

# Or just add the option at the end (last value wins for most options)
fzf --height=90%   # overrides the height set in FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS

items.title: Checking the Effective Configuration
items.text:
# See what fzf would do with current environment
fzf --bash | grep -A5 'CTRL_T'

# Print fzf version
fzf --version

items.title: Using with tmux
items.text:
# Drop-in tmux popup instead of inline
export FZF_TMUX=1
export FZF_TMUX_OPTS="-p 80%,60%"   # popup: 80% wide, 60% tall

# Or use fzf-tmux directly
git branch | fzf-tmux -p 60%

items.title: fzf — Tool Integrations
items.text: fzf integrates cleanly with editors, version control, package managers, and container tools.

items.title: fzf.vim plugin
items.text:
Install junegunn/fzf and junegunn/fzf.vim. Commands available:

Command Description :Files [PATH] Fuzzy-find files :GFiles Files tracked by git :GFiles? Git status (changed files) :Buffers Open buffers :Colors Color schemes :Ag [PATTERN] ag search results :Rg [PATTERN] ripgrep results (live) :Lines Lines in open buffers :BLines Lines in current buffer :Tags Tags in the project :BTags Tags in current buffer :Marks Marks :Jumps Jump list :History v:oldfiles + open buffers :History: Command history :History/ Search history :Commits Git commits (requires fugitive) :BCommits Git commits for current buffer :Commands Vim commands :Maps Key mappings :Helptags Help tags :Filetypes File types 
" Recommended key mappings in .vimrc / init.vim
nnoremap <C-p> :Files<CR>
nnoremap <leader>b :Buffers<CR>
nnoremap <leader>rg :Rg<CR>
nnoremap <leader>/ :BLines<CR>

items.title: Neovim: telescope.nvim
items.text:
telescope.nvim is the dominant Neovim fuzzy finder. Uses the same concepts as fzf but is native Lua with a richer UI.

-- telescope keymaps (init.lua)
local builtin = require('telescope.builtin')
vim.keymap.set('n', '<C-p>', builtin.find_files)
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>rg', builtin.live_grep)
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>b', builtin.buffers)
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>h', builtin.help_tags)

items.title: Neovim: fzf-lua
items.text:
fzf-lua is a fast, fzf-backed alternative to telescope. Uses the actual fzf binary.

require('fzf-lua').setup({ winopts = { height = 0.85, width = 0.80 } })
vim.keymap.set('n', '<C-p>', require('fzf-lua').files)
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>rg', require('fzf-lua').live_grep)

items.title: Interactively stage hunks
items.text: git diff --name-only | fzf -m | xargs git add

items.title: Cherry-pick from log
items.text:
git log --oneline | fzf --preview 'git show {1}' \
  | awk '{print $1}' | xargs git cherry-pick

items.title: Interactively restore a file to HEAD
items.text:
git diff --name-only HEAD | fzf \
  --preview 'git diff --color=always HEAD -- {}' \
  | xargs git restore

items.title: Browse tags
items.text: git tag | fzf --preview 'git show {}'

items.title: Select a tmux session to switch to
items.text:
ftmux-session() {
  local session
  session=$(tmux list-sessions -F '#{session_name}' \
    | fzf --preview 'tmux list-windows -t {}' \
          --prompt="Session: ")
  [ -n "$session" ] && tmux switch-client -t "$session"
}

items.title: Open a project in a new tmux window
items.text:
fproject() {
  local dir
  dir=$(fd --type d --max-depth 3 . ~/projects ~/work 2>/dev/null \
    | fzf --preview 'ls {}' \
          --prompt="Project: ")
  [ -n "$dir" ] && tmux new-window -c "$dir" -n "$(basename "$dir")"
}

items.title: fzf inside a tmux popup
items.text:
# Any fzf command can be wrapped in a tmux popup
git branch | fzf-tmux -p 60%,40% --preview 'git log --oneline {-1}'

items.title: Homebrew — install formula interactively
items.text: brew install $(brew search | fzf --preview 'brew info {}')

items.title: Homebrew — uninstall interactively
items.text: brew uninstall $(brew list | fzf -m --preview 'brew info {}')

items.title: Homebrew — install cask
items.text: brew install --cask $(brew search --casks | fzf --preview 'brew info --cask {}')

items.title: apt / dnf — install interactively
items.text:
# apt
apt-cache search '' | fzf | awk '{print $1}' | xargs sudo apt install -y

# dnf
dnf list available 2>/dev/null | fzf | awk '{print $1}' | xargs sudo dnf install -y

items.title: Live grep with fzf (see also fzf recipes)
items.text:
rg --color=always --line-number '' \
  | fzf --ansi --delimiter=: \
        --preview 'bat --color=always {1} --highlight-line {2}' \
        --preview-window='right:60%:+{2}+3/3' \
        --bind 'enter:execute(vim +{2} {1})'

items.title: SPIRE / Kubernetes (VKS)
items.text:
# Select a SPIFFE SVID workload
fsvid() {
  local entry
  entry=$(spire-server entry show -output json 2>/dev/null \
    | jq -r '.entries[] | "\(.id.value)\t\(.spiffe_id.value)"' \
    | fzf --delimiter='\t' --with-nth=2 \
          --preview 'spire-server entry show -entryID {1} 2>/dev/null | jq .')
  [ -n "$entry" ] && echo "$entry" | awk '{print $1}'
}

items.title: fzf — Key Bindings & Actions
items.text: fzf's --bind option lets you attach any action (or chain of actions) to any key.

items.title: Default Key Bindings
items.text: Key Action Enter Confirm selection Ctrl-C / Esc Abort ↑ / Ctrl-K / Ctrl-P Move cursor up ↓ / Ctrl-J / Ctrl-N Move cursor down Tab Toggle selection mark (multi-select mode) Shift-Tab Toggle selection and move up Ctrl-A Select all (if bound) Ctrl-D Deselect all (if bound) Page Up / Page Down Scroll page Home / End Jump to first/last item Ctrl-F / Ctrl-B Scroll preview page down/up (if bound) Alt-A Select all matching items Ctrl-R Toggle sort Ctrl-/ or ? Toggle preview (if bound)

items.title: --bind Syntax
items.text:
--bind 'KEY:ACTION'
--bind 'KEY:ACTION1+ACTION2'        # chain multiple actions
--bind 'KEY1:ACTION,KEY2:ACTION'    # multiple bindings in one --bind

items.title: Key Names
items.text: ctrl-[a-z], alt-[a-zA-Z], f1–f12, enter, esc, tab, btab (shift-tab), space, bspace, up, down, left, right, home, end, pgup, pgdn, insert, delete, double-click, left-click, right-click, scroll-up, scroll-down, change (query changed), start, load, result (result updated), focus (focus changed)

items.title: Navigation
items.text: Action Description up / down Move cursor page-up / page-down Move by page half-page-up / half-page-down Move by half page first / last Jump to first/last item jump EasyMotion-style jump (requires --jump-labels) jump-accept Jump and accept in one action

items.title: Selection
items.text: Action Description select / deselect Select/deselect current item toggle Toggle selection of current item select-all / deselect-all Select/deselect all toggle-all Toggle all selections

items.title: Preview
items.text: Action Description toggle-preview Show/hide preview window toggle-preview-wrap Toggle line wrapping in preview preview-up / preview-down Scroll preview by one line preview-page-up / preview-page-down Scroll preview by page preview-half-page-up / preview-half-page-down Scroll by half page preview-top / preview-bottom Jump to top/bottom of preview change-preview(CMD) Change the preview command change-preview-window(OPTS) Cycle through window configurations refresh-preview Re-run the preview command

items.title: Input
items.text: Action Description clear-query Clear the query string clear-screen Redraw the screen kill-line Delete from cursor to end of line kill-word Delete word before cursor unix-line-discard Delete from cursor to beginning unix-word-rubout Delete word before cursor (unix-style) yank Paste from kill buffer backward-delete-char Delete character before cursor delete-char Delete character at cursor backward-char / forward-char Move cursor left/right beginning-of-line / end-of-line Jump to start/end of query backward-word / forward-word Move by word

items.title: Execution
items.text: Action Description execute(CMD) Run CMD with selected item; stay in fzf execute-silent(CMD) Run CMD without showing output become(CMD) Replace fzf process with CMD reload(CMD) Re-run CMD and refresh the list reload-sync(CMD) Like reload but waits for completion

items.title: Other
items.text: Action Description accept Accept selection (like Enter) accept-non-empty Accept only if something is selected abort Quit without output print-query Print the query and exit replace-query Replace query with current item toggle-sort Toggle sort on/off toggle-search Enable/disable search offset-up / offset-down Scroll the view without moving cursor change-header(STR) Change the header text dynamically unbind(KEY) Remove a binding rebind(KEY) Restore a binding

items.title: Practical --bind Examples
items.text:
# Open file in vim on enter, keep fzf open after
fzf --bind 'enter:execute(vim {})+reload(ls)'

# Copy path to clipboard (macOS pbcopy / Linux xclip)
fzf --bind 'ctrl-y:execute-silent(echo -n {} | pbcopy)'
fzf --bind 'ctrl-y:execute-silent(echo -n {} | xclip -selection clipboard)'

# Open with system default app
fzf --bind 'ctrl-o:execute-silent(xdg-open {})'

# Reload list after deleting a file
fzf --bind 'ctrl-x:execute(rm {})+reload(ls)'

# Toggle all and accept
fzf -m --bind 'ctrl-a:select-all+accept'

# Dynamic preview content based on query
fzf --preview 'grep -i {q} {} 2>/dev/null | head -20' \
    --bind 'change:refresh-preview'

# Cycle preview window layouts
fzf --preview 'cat {}' \
    --bind 'ctrl-/:change-preview-window(right:60%|down:40%|hidden|)'

# Become: replace fzf with the command (no subshell overhead)
fzf --bind 'enter:become(vim {})'

# Multiple actions chained with +
fzf --bind 'ctrl-r:toggle-sort+clear-screen'

execute vs execute-silent vs become

  • execute(CMD) — runs CMD, shows its output, returns to fzf when done
  • execute-silent(CMD) — runs CMD in background, doesn't interrupt the UI
  • become(CMD) — replaces the fzf process entirely (like exec); most efficient for the "open in editor" pattern

items.title: fzf — Key Options
items.text: All options can be set on the command line or in FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS.

items.title: Layout & Appearance
items.text: Option Description --height=N[%] Show fzf inline at N rows or N% of terminal height instead of fullscreen --min-height=N Minimum height when using percentage --layout=LAYOUT default (bottom-up), reverse (top-down), reverse-list --border[=STYLE] Draw a border. Styles: rounded, sharp, bold, double, horizontal, vertical, top, bottom, left, right, none --border-label=STR Label text displayed in the border --border-label-pos=N Position of border label (positive = from left, negative = from right) --margin=TRBL Margin around the fzf window (e.g., 1,2) --padding=TRBL Inner padding --info=STYLE Display style for match count: default, right, hidden, inline --separator=STR Separator between the header and the list --scrollbar[=C] Scrollbar character --color=OPTS Customize colors (see below)

items.title: Prompt & Header
items.text: Option Description --prompt=STR Input prompt string (default: >) --pointer=STR Character for the current item (default: >) --marker=STR Character for selected items in multi-select (default: >) --header=STR Static header text shown above the list --header-lines=N Treat first N lines of input as a sticky, non-filterable header --header-first Show header before the prompt

items.title: Input & Output
items.text: Option Description -m, --multi Enable multi-select (Tab = toggle, Enter = confirm all) --no-multi Disable multi-select (default) --query=STR Start with this initial query -1, --select-1 If only one match, auto-select without showing UI -0, --exit-0 Exit immediately with no output if no match --filter=STR Non-interactive filter mode: just output matches and exit --print-query Print the query string as the first line of output --expect=KEYS Print the key used to exit as first line (ctrl-v, enter, etc.) --read0 Read null-delimited input (for filenames with spaces/newlines) --print0 Output null-delimited results --no-sort Don't sort results; preserve input order --tac Reverse the input before displaying --sync Wait until all input is read before displaying --tail=N Only display the last N items

items.title: Field Handling
items.text:
Option Description --delimiter=STR Field delimiter for --nth and --with-nth (string or regex) --nth=FIELDS Comma-separated list of fields to search. Use -1 for last field. --with-nth=FIELDS Display only these fields (full line still stored internally) --ansi Parse ANSI color codes in input and render them 
# Search only filename (last component of a path)
find . -type f | fzf --delimiter=/ --nth=-1

# Show only filename, but pass full path downstream
find . -type f | fzf --delimiter=/ --with-nth=-1

# Parse colored output
ls --color=always | fzf --ansi

items.title: Search Behavior
items.text: Option Description -i, --ignore-case Case-insensitive matching +i, --no-ignore-case Case-sensitive matching --exact Use exact matching by default (no fuzzy) +x, --no-extended Disable extended search (fuzzy only) --algo=TYPE Scoring algorithm: v1 (fast) or v2 (default, accurate) --disabled Start with search disabled (useful with dynamic reload) --keep-right Keep the right side of long lines visible in the list --filepath-word Make word-wise actions (alt-b, alt-f) respect path separators

items.title: Practical Option Combinations
items.text:
# Quick picker: inline, no fullscreen
fzf --height=40% --layout=reverse --border=rounded

# kubectl-style: show header, search everything
kubectl get pods | fzf --header-lines=1 --height=50%

# File picker with preview and copy-path binding
fzf --preview 'bat --color=always {}' \
    --bind 'ctrl-y:execute-silent(echo -n {} | pbcopy)' \
    --header 'Ctrl-Y: copy path'

# Non-interactive filter (use in scripts like grep)
fzf --filter="pattern" < list.txt

# Multi-select with select-all binding
fzf -m --bind 'ctrl-a:select-all,ctrl-d:deselect-all'

# Pass a key to detect how user exited
result=$(fzf --expect=ctrl-v,ctrl-x,enter)
key=$(head -1 <<< "$result")
sel=$(tail -1 <<< "$result")
case "$key" in
  ctrl-v) vim "$sel" ;;
  ctrl-x) xdg-open "$sel" ;;
  *)      echo "$sel" ;;
esac

items.title: Color Customization
items.text:
# Format: --color=COMPONENT:ANSI_CODE,...
# or use named themes: dark, light, 16, bw

# Components: fg, bg, hl (match highlight), fg+, bg+, hl+,
#   info, border, prompt, pointer, marker, spinner, header, label

fzf --color='bg:#0c0e13,bg+:#1e232e,fg:#c9d1e0,fg+:#e8edf5'  \
    --color='hl:#5ac8a0,hl+:#5ac8a0,info:#f0a05a,prompt:#9b7de8' \
    --color='pointer:#9b7de8,marker:#5ac8a0,border:#252b38'

items.title: fzf — Preview Window
items.text: The preview window runs a shell command for each highlighted item and shows the output in a side pane — in real time as you move through the list. It's one of fzf's most powerful features.

items.title: Basic Usage
items.text:
# Preview file content (cat fallback if bat not available)
fzf --preview 'bat --color=always {} 2>/dev/null || cat {}'

# Preview directory listing
find . -type d | fzf --preview 'ls -la {}'

# Preview git log for each branch
git branch | fzf --preview 'git log --oneline --color=always {-1} | head -20'

items.title: Preview Placeholders
items.text: Placeholder Expands to {} The current line (full text of highlighted item) {1}, {2}, … Field N (using --delimiter) {-1} Last field {1..3} Fields 1 through 3 {2..} From field 2 to last {..3} From first field to field 3 {q} Current query string {n} Zero-indexed line number of the item in the list {+} All selected items (space-separated, multi-select) {+1} Field 1 of all selected items

items.title: Window Position and Size
items.text:
# Right side, 60% wide (default)
fzf --preview 'cat {}' --preview-window=right:60%

# Top, 40% height
fzf --preview 'cat {}' --preview-window=up:40%

# Bottom, 20 lines, word-wrap
fzf --preview 'cat {}' --preview-window=bottom:20:wrap

# Left side, no border
fzf --preview 'cat {}' --preview-window=left:40%:noborder

# Hidden by default (toggle with a key)
fzf --preview 'cat {}' --preview-window=hidden \
    --bind '?:toggle-preview'

items.title: Size format
items.text: [POSITION][:SIZE][:OPTS] where: - POSITION: up, down, left, right (default: right) - SIZE: N lines or N% of terminal - OPTS: wrap, border, noborder, follow, cycle, hidden, nofollow

items.title: Scrolling the Preview
items.text:
# Auto-scroll to bottom (useful for logs)
fzf --preview 'tail -50 {}' --preview-window=follow

# Key bindings to scroll manually
fzf --preview 'cat {}' \
    --bind 'ctrl-f:preview-page-down' \
    --bind 'ctrl-b:preview-page-up' \
    --bind 'ctrl-u:preview-half-page-up' \
    --bind 'ctrl-d:preview-half-page-down'

items.title: Toggle and Cycle Preview Size
items.text:
# Toggle preview on/off
fzf --preview 'cat {}' --bind '?:toggle-preview'

# Cycle through sizes
fzf --preview 'cat {}' \
    --bind 'ctrl-/:change-preview-window(right:60%|down:40%|hidden|)'

items.title: Syntax-Highlighted File Preview
items.text:
# With bat (best option)
fzf --preview 'bat --color=always --style=numbers --line-range=:200 {}'

# With highlight
fzf --preview 'highlight -O ansi {} 2>/dev/null || cat {}'

# With cat (POSIX fallback)
fzf --preview 'cat {}'

items.title: Preview With Line Context (Grep Results)
items.text:
# grep -n output: FILE:LINE:CONTENT
grep -rn "TODO" . | fzf \
  --delimiter=: \
  --preview 'bat --color=always --highlight-line {2} {1}' \
  --preview-window 'right:60%:+{2}+3/3'

# The +{2}+3/3 scroll expression means:
#   Start at line {2} (the match), offset by 3, using 1/3 of preview height as context

items.title: Dynamic Preview Based on File Type
items.text:
fzf --preview '
  file={};
  case "$file" in
    *.json) jq . "$file" ;;
    *.yaml|*.yml) cat "$file" ;;
    *.png|*.jpg) echo "[image: $file]" ;;
    *) bat --color=always "$file" 2>/dev/null || cat "$file" ;;
  esac
'

items.title: Preview With the Current Query
items.text:
# Show grep results for the current query in the preview
fzf --preview 'grep --color=always -i {q} {} 2>/dev/null | head -20' \
    --bind 'change:reload(find . -type f)'

items.title: Live Grep with Reload (rg + fzf)
items.text:
# Start with search disabled; reload rg on every keystroke
fzf --disabled --ansi \
    --bind 'start:reload:rg --color=always --line-number "" .' \
    --bind 'change:reload:rg --color=always --line-number {q} . || true' \
    --delimiter=: \
    --preview 'bat --color=always --highlight-line {2} {1}' \
    --preview-window 'right:60%:+{2}+3/3' \
    --bind 'enter:execute(vim +{2} {1})'

Performance

Preview commands run in a subshell on every cursor move. For expensive operations, add a short delay with --preview-window=...:~3 (show first 3 lines as a placeholder while command runs), or gate expensive commands behind a key binding using execute instead of --preview.

items.title: fzf — Practical Recipes
items.text: Shell functions you can drop straight into ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc. Each one is self-contained.

items.title: Interactive branch checkout
items.text:
fbr() {
  local branch
  branch=$(git branch -a \
    | grep -v HEAD \
    | sed 's/^[* ]*//' \
    | fzf --preview "git log --oneline --color=always {-1} | head -20" \
          --preview-window=right:50% \
          --prompt="Branch: ")
  [ -n "$branch" ] && git checkout "${branch#remotes/origin/}"
}

items.title: Interactive git log browser
items.text:
fgl() {
  git log --oneline --color=always "$@" \
    | fzf --ansi --no-sort --reverse \
          --preview 'git show --color=always {1}' \
          --preview-window=right:60% \
          --bind 'enter:execute(git show --color=always {1} | less -R)' \
          --bind 'ctrl-y:execute-silent(echo {1} | pbcopy)' \
          --header='Enter: show diff  Ctrl-Y: copy hash'
}

items.title: Interactive git stash apply
items.text:
fstash() {
  local stash
  stash=$(git stash list \
    | fzf --preview 'git stash show -p {1}' \
          --preview-window=right:60%)
  [ -n "$stash" ] && git stash apply "${stash%%:*}"
}

items.title: Interactively stage files
items.text:
fadd() {
  local files
  files=$(git diff --name-only HEAD \
    | fzf -m \
          --preview 'git diff --color=always HEAD -- {}' \
          --preview-window=right:60%)
  [ -n "$files" ] && echo "$files" | xargs git add
}

items.title: Checkout a file from a specific commit
items.text:
fco() {
  local commit file
  commit=$(git log --oneline | fzf --preview 'git show {1}' | awk '{print $1}')
  file=$(git show --stat "$commit" | grep '|' | awk '{print $1}' \
    | fzf --preview "git show $commit -- {}")
  [ -n "$file" ] && git checkout "$commit" -- "$file"
}

items.title: Kill a process interactively
items.text:
fkill() {
  local pid
  pid=$(ps aux \
    | fzf --header-lines=1 \
          --preview 'echo {}' \
          --preview-window=down:3:wrap \
          --prompt="Kill: " \
    | awk '{print $2}')
  [ -n "$pid" ] && kill -"${1:-9}" "$pid"
}

items.title: Interactive lsof port checker
items.text:
fport() {
  local entry
  entry=$(sudo lsof -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -P \
    | fzf --header-lines=1 \
          --prompt="Port: ")
  [ -n "$entry" ] && echo "$entry" | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill -9
}

items.title: Open recent files (from shell history patterns)
items.text:
fhist() {
  local file
  file=$(history \
    | grep -oE '[^ ]+\.(go|yaml|md|sh|py|json|tf)' \
    | sort -u \
    | fzf --preview 'bat --color=always {} 2>/dev/null' \
          --preview-window=right:60%)
  [ -n "$file" ] && ${EDITOR:-vim} "$file"
}

items.title: Copy file path(s) to clipboard
items.text:
fcopy() {
  fzf -m \
    --preview 'bat --color=always {}' \
    --preview-window=right:50% \
  | tr '\n' ' ' | pbcopy   # macOS; replace pbcopy with xclip -sel clip for Linux
}

items.title: Interactive container exec
items.text:
dexec() {
  local ctr shell="${1:-/bin/sh}"
  ctr=$(docker ps --format '{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}' \
    | fzf --header='NAME    IMAGE    STATUS' \
          --preview 'docker inspect {1} | jq .[0].Config' \
          --delimiter='\t' \
          --with-nth=1,2,3 \
    | awk '{print $1}')
  [ -n "$ctr" ] && docker exec -it "$ctr" "$shell"
}

items.title: Interactive image removal
items.text:
drmi() {
  docker images \
    | fzf -m --header-lines=1 \
          --preview 'docker inspect {3}' \
    | awk '{print $3}' \
    | xargs docker rmi
}

items.title: Tail container logs
items.text:
dlogs() {
  local ctr
  ctr=$(docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | fzf --prompt="Container: ")
  [ -n "$ctr" ] && docker logs -f "$ctr"
}

items.title: Interactive pod log viewer
items.text:
klogs() {
  local ns pod
  ns=$(kubectl get ns -o name | cut -d/ -f2 \
    | fzf --prompt="Namespace: " --preview 'kubectl get pods -n {}')
  [ -z "$ns" ] && return
  pod=$(kubectl get pods -n "$ns" -o name | cut -d/ -f2 \
    | fzf --prompt="Pod: " \
          --preview "kubectl describe pod -n $ns {}")
  [ -n "$pod" ] && kubectl logs -f -n "$ns" "$pod"
}

items.title: Interactive pod exec
items.text:
kexec() {
  local ns pod shell="${1:-/bin/sh}"
  ns=$(kubectl get ns -o name | cut -d/ -f2 | fzf --prompt="Namespace: ")
  [ -z "$ns" ] && return
  pod=$(kubectl get pods -n "$ns" -o name | cut -d/ -f2 \
    | fzf --prompt="Pod: " \
          --preview "kubectl describe pod -n $ns {}")
  [ -n "$pod" ] && kubectl exec -it -n "$ns" "$pod" -- "$shell"
}

items.title: Port-forward to a service
items.text:
kpf() {
  local entry ns svc port
  entry=$(kubectl get svc -A \
    | fzf --header-lines=1 \
          --preview 'kubectl describe svc -n {1} {2}')
  [ -z "$entry" ] && return
  ns=$(echo "$entry" | awk '{print $1}')
  svc=$(echo "$entry" | awk '{print $2}')
  read -rp "Local port: " port
  kubectl port-forward -n "$ns" "svc/$svc" "${port}:${port}"
}

items.title: Pick a host from ~/.ssh/config
items.text:
fssh() {
  local host
  host=$(grep -E '^Host [^*]' ~/.ssh/config \
    | awk '{print $2}' \
    | fzf --prompt="SSH: " \
          --preview 'grep -A10 "^Host {}" ~/.ssh/config')
  [ -n "$host" ] && ssh "$host"
}

items.title: Interactive Live Grep (ripgrep + fzf)
items.text:
The most powerful recipe: a live search that updates as you type, with file preview.

rgi() {
  local initial_query="${*:-}"
  : | fzf \
    --disabled \
    --ansi \
    --query "$initial_query" \
    --bind "start:reload:rg --color=always --line-number --no-heading -- {q} . || true" \
    --bind "change:reload:rg --color=always --line-number --no-heading -- {q} . || true" \
    --bind 'enter:execute(vim +{1} {2..})' \
    --delimiter=: \
    --preview 'bat --color=always {2} --highlight-line {1}' \
    --preview-window 'right:60%:+{1}+3/3' \
    --prompt='Search: '
}

Usage: rgi or rgi "initial search term"

items.title: fzf — Search Syntax
items.text: fzf uses its own extended search syntax (enabled by default with --extended, which is on by default). Multiple terms are separated by spaces and act as AND.

items.title: Token Types
items.text: Token Match Type Description sbtrkt fuzzy Items that fuzzy-match "sbtrkt" (any chars between) 'word exact Items that include "word" as an exact substring ^prefix prefix-exact Items that start with "prefix" suffix$ suffix-exact Items that end with "suffix" !word inverse-exact Items that do NOT include "word" !^prefix inverse-prefix Items that do NOT start with "prefix" !suffix$ inverse-suffix Items that do NOT end with "suffix" term1 term2 AND Items matching both terms (space = AND) term1 \| term2 OR Items matching either term (pipe = OR)

items.title: Examples
items.text:
# Fuzzy: type partial characters in any order
fzf   # type: cfg   → matches "config", "cfg.yaml", etc.

# Exact: must contain the literal string
fzf   # type: 'test  → matches lines with "test" exactly

# Prefix: must start with "main"
fzf   # type: ^main

# Suffix: must end with ".go"
fzf   # type: .go$

# Inverse: exclude lines containing "vendor"
fzf   # type: !vendor

# AND: .go files that are NOT test files
fzf   # type: .go$ !_test

# AND: files starting with "cmd" and containing "main"
fzf   # type: ^cmd 'main

# OR: files ending in .go OR .sh
fzf   # type: .go$ | .sh$

# Complex: go files, not vendor, not test
fzf   # type: .go$ !vendor !_test

items.title: Case Sensitivity
items.text:
By default, fzf is smart-case: lowercase query = case-insensitive, uppercase query = case-sensitive.

# Force case-insensitive
fzf -i
fzf --ignore-case

# Force case-sensitive
fzf +i
fzf --no-ignore-case

items.title: Disabling Extended Search
items.text:
# Pure fuzzy mode only (no exact/prefix/suffix/inverse tokens)
fzf +x
fzf --no-extended

# Force all searches to be exact
fzf --exact

items.title: Scoping Search with --nth
items.text:
By default fzf searches the whole line. Use --nth to restrict matching to specific fields.

# Search only on the filename (last slash-delimited field)
find . -type f | fzf --delimiter=/ --nth=-1

# Search only on fields 2 and 3 (tab-delimited)
cat data.tsv | fzf --delimiter='\t' --nth=2,3

# Display only field 2 but search the whole line
cat data.txt | fzf --with-nth=2

items.title: Scoring
items.text:
fzf scores matches using an algorithm that prefers:

  1. Consecutive matching characters over scattered ones
  2. Characters at word boundaries (after /, _, -, ., space)
  3. Characters at the start of the string
  4. Shorter strings (fewer total characters)

This means typing a partial filename like mkd will score mkdocs.yml higher than a long path that happens to contain those letters scattered throughout.

# Use the v1 algorithm (faster, slightly less accurate)
fzf --algo=v1

# Default: v2 (Smith-Waterman variant)
fzf --algo=v2

items.title: fzf — Shell Integration
items.text: fzf's shell integration installs several key bindings and a fuzzy completion trigger. Enable it once in your shell config and you'll wonder how you lived without it.

items.title: Enable Integration
items.text:
# ~/.bashrc
eval "$(fzf --bash)"

# ~/.zshrc
eval "$(fzf --zsh)"

# ~/.config/fish/config.fish
fzf --fish | source

items.title: Ctrl-T — Paste Selected Files
items.text:
Invokes find (or FZF_CTRL_T_COMMAND) and pastes the selected path(s) at the cursor.

# Type a command, then press Ctrl-T to insert a file path
vim <Ctrl-T>            # select a file to open
git add <Ctrl-T>        # select files to stage
cp <Ctrl-T> /dest/      # select source file

items.title: Ctrl-R — Interactive History Search
items.text:
Replaces the default Ctrl-R reverse-incremental-search with fzf. Much faster and shows context.

# Press Ctrl-R at any prompt
# Type to fuzzy-search your shell history
# Enter to execute, or Ctrl-C to paste without executing (bash/zsh differ)

items.title: Alt-C — Fuzzy cd
items.text:
Invokes find (or FZF_ALT_C_COMMAND) to select a directory, then cds into it.

# Press Alt-C at any prompt
# Type to fuzzy-search subdirectories
# Enter to cd into the selected directory

items.title: Fuzzy Completion with **
items.text:
Type ** followed by Tab to trigger fzf completion for the current command.

vim **<Tab>             # select a file to open
cd **<Tab>              # select a directory
kill -9 **<Tab>         # select a process ID
ssh **<Tab>             # select a host from ~/.ssh/config
unset **<Tab>           # select an environment variable to unset
export **<Tab>          # select an environment variable

The trigger string is configurable:

export FZF_COMPLETION_TRIGGER='~~'   # use ~~ instead of **

items.title: Customizing Shell Bindings
items.text: All bindings are controlled via environment variables. Put these in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc.

items.title: Ctrl-T customization
items.text:
# Use fd instead of find (respects .gitignore, faster)
export FZF_CTRL_T_COMMAND="fd --type f --hidden --follow --exclude .git"

# Add preview to Ctrl-T
export FZF_CTRL_T_OPTS="
  --preview 'bat --color=always --line-range=:50 {}'
  --bind 'ctrl-/:toggle-preview'
  --border=rounded"

items.title: Ctrl-R customization
items.text:
export FZF_CTRL_R_OPTS="
  --preview 'echo {}'
  --preview-window up:3:hidden:wrap
  --bind 'ctrl-/:toggle-preview'
  --bind 'ctrl-y:execute-silent(echo -n {2..} | pbcopy)+abort'
  --color header:italic
  --header 'Ctrl-Y: copy to clipboard'"

items.title: Alt-C customization
items.text:
# Use fd for directory search
export FZF_ALT_C_COMMAND="fd --type d --hidden --follow --exclude .git"

# Show tree preview
export FZF_ALT_C_OPTS="
  --preview 'tree -C {} | head -50'
  --border=rounded"

items.title: Custom Completion for Specific Commands
items.text:
Define _fzf_comprun to provide custom fzf options per command:

_fzf_comprun() {
  local command=$1
  shift

  case "$command" in
    cd)           fzf --preview 'tree -C {} | head -30' "$@" ;;
    export|unset) fzf --preview "eval 'echo \$'{}" "$@" ;;
    ssh)          fzf --preview 'dig {}' "$@" ;;
    *)            fzf --preview 'bat --color=always {} 2>/dev/null || ls {}' "$@" ;;
  esac
}

items.title: Custom Path and Directory Generators
items.text:
Override _fzf_compgen_path and _fzf_compgen_dir to change what appears in ** completion:

# Use fd for both path and directory completion
_fzf_compgen_path() {
  fd --hidden --follow --exclude ".git" . "$1"
}

_fzf_compgen_dir() {
  fd --type d --hidden --follow --exclude ".git" . "$1"
}

items.title: Disabling Specific Bindings
items.text:
# Disable Alt-C (if it conflicts with another binding)
export FZF_ALT_C_COMMAND=""

# Or bind it to something else
bindkey -r '\ec'    # zsh: remove the binding entirely

zsh widget conflicts

On zsh, if Ctrl-T or Alt-C don't work, check bindkey | grep fzf and bindkey | grep ctrl. Some plugin frameworks (oh-my-zsh, antidote) bind these keys themselves. The fzf shell integration must be sourced after those frameworks.

items.title: sed — Stream Editor
items.text: sed reads input line by line into the pattern space, applies commands, prints the pattern space, and loops. The mental model: it's a loop + a buffer + a script.

items.title: How sed Works
items.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  For each line of input:                                 │
│                                                          │
│  1. Read line into PATTERN SPACE                         │
│  2. Execute all commands in the script                   │
│  3. (Unless -n) print pattern space                      │
│  4. Clear pattern space, go to next line                 │
│                                                          │
│  HOLD SPACE persists across cycles (starts empty)        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

items.title: Invocation
items.text:
sed [OPTIONS] 'SCRIPT' [FILE...]
sed [OPTIONS] -e 'CMD1' -e 'CMD2' [FILE...]
sed [OPTIONS] -f script.sed [FILE...]

items.title: Options
items.text: Option Meaning -n Suppress automatic printing; only print when explicitly told to (p command) -e 'script' Add a script expression (multiple -e allowed) -f file Read script from a file -i[SUFFIX] Edit file in-place; optional suffix creates a backup (-i.bak) -E / -r Use extended regular expressions (ERE) — enables +, ?, |, (), {} without backslash -s Treat each file separately (line numbers reset per file) --sandbox GNU sed: disallow r/w/e commands (safe mode)

items.title: Command structure
items.text:
A sed command has the form:

[address[,address]]command[flags]

macOS vs GNU sed

macOS ships BSD sed. For full GNU sed behavior — especially -i without a suffix — install gnu-sed via Homebrew (brew install gnu-sed) and invoke as gsed, or prepend it to your PATH.

items.title: Sections
items.text: Page Contents Addressing Line numbers, regexes, ranges, negation Substitution The s command — flags, backreferences, case conversion Core Commands d p q a i c y n N h H g G x and more Pattern & Hold Space The two buffers and how to use them Multi-line N, P, D and the sliding window idiom Branching :label, b, t, T — loops inside sed In-place Editing -i, backups, recursive replacement Recipes Real-world one-liners and scripts

items.title: sed — Addressing Lines
items.text: An address tells sed which lines to apply a command to. Without an address, the command applies to every line.

items.title: Address Forms
items.text: Address Form Matches N Line number N (1-based) $ Last line 0 GNU: used as start of range with regex (0,/re/ matches from start even if line 1 matches) /regex/ Any line matching regex \cregexc Regex with delimiter c instead of / (useful when pattern contains /) first~step GNU: every step-th line starting at first (e.g., 1~2 = odd lines) addr1,addr2 Range from addr1 to addr2 inclusive addr1,+N addr1 and the next N lines addr1,~N GNU: addr1 up to next multiple of N addr! Negate: apply to lines NOT matching addr

items.title: Single line
items.text:
# Apply only to line 3
sed '3s/foo/bar/' file

# Apply to the last line
sed '$s/foo/bar/' file

items.title: Ranges
items.text:
# Lines 5 through 10
sed '5,10s/foo/bar/' file

# From line matching /START/ to line matching /END/
sed '/START/,/END/s/foo/bar/' file

# From line 3 and the next 4 lines (lines 3-7)
sed '3,+4s/foo/bar/' file

# From first match to EOF (open range)
sed '/BEGIN/,$p' file

items.title: Negation
items.text:
# All lines EXCEPT line 1 (skip header)
sed '1!s/foo/bar/' file

# All lines NOT matching a pattern
sed '/^#/!s/foo/bar/' file

items.title: GNU step addressing
items.text:
# Odd lines: 1, 3, 5, ...
sed '1~2s/foo/bar/' file

# Even lines: 2, 4, 6, ...
sed '0~2s/foo/bar/' file

# Every 3rd line starting at line 2
sed '2~3s/foo/bar/' file

items.title: Alternate regex delimiter
items.text:
# Use | instead of / — handy when pattern contains /
sed '\|/usr/local|d' paths.txt

items.title: Blocks: Multiple Commands on One Address
items.text:
# Apply two commands only to lines with "ERROR"
sed '/ERROR/{
  s/ERROR/CRITICAL/
  s/$/ <<< ALERT/
}' logfile

# Same on one line with semicolons
sed '/ERROR/{s/ERROR/CRITICAL/;s/$/ <<< ALERT/}' logfile

Range Trap

A range /START/,/END/ activates when the first regex matches and deactivates after the second matches. If END never matches, the range stays open until EOF. If START and END are on the same line, the range opens and then immediately closes at the next END match — which may be the same line.

0,/regex/ vs 1,/regex/

Use 0,/re/ (GNU) when you want the range to work even if the very first line matches the pattern. 1,/re/ would not close on line 1 because the end address is only tested starting from line 2.

items.title: sed — Branching & Labels
items.text: sed has a minimal loop construct via :label, b (unconditional branch), t (branch if substitution succeeded), and T (branch if substitution failed, GNU).

items.title: Branch Commands
items.text: Command Behavior : label Define a label (jump target). Label name is alphanumeric. b [label] Unconditionally jump to label. If no label, jump to end of script (skip remaining commands for this cycle). t [label] Jump to label only if a s/// command has succeeded since the last input line was read or since the last t. T [label] Jump to label only if NO s/// has succeeded. GNU extension; inverse of t.

items.title: Loop Until No More Matches
items.text:
The classic use of t: keep applying a substitution until it no longer matches.

# Remove all leading whitespace (loop until none left)
sed ':loop; s/^[[:space:]]//; t loop' file.txt

# Remove all trailing whitespace
sed ':loop; s/[[:space:]]$//; t loop' file.txt

# Collapse multiple spaces into one (loop is cleaner than just /g)
sed ':loop; s/  / /; t loop' file.txt
# (though s/  */ /g is more efficient for this)

items.title: Loop Over Multi-line Content
items.text:
# Join continuation lines (lines ending with \)
sed ':a; /\\$/ { N; s/\\\n//; ba }' file.txt

# Join lines ending with a comma
sed ':a; /,$/ { N; s/,\n/,/; ba }' file.txt

# Remove multi-line C block comments
sed -E ':a; s|/\*[^*]*\*/||g; ta; /\/\*/ { N; ba }' file.c

items.title: Skip Lines with b
items.text:
Use b (branch to end of script) to skip all remaining commands for matching lines.

# Skip comment lines — don't process lines starting with #
sed '/^#/b; s/foo/bar/g' config.txt

# Skip header line in CSV
sed '1b; s/,/\t/g' data.csv

# Skip empty lines, process the rest
sed '/^$/b; s/^/  /' file.txt

items.title: GNU T: Act Only When s/// Failed
items.text:
# Tag lines where substitution did NOT occur
sed 's/ERROR/FOUND/; T skip; s/$/ <<< ALERT/; :skip' log

# Remove lines where a substitution could not be made
sed 's/[0-9]/X/g; T delete; b; :delete; d' file.txt

items.title: Word-wrap Long Lines (crude)
items.text:
# Insert a newline after every 72nd character
sed ':a; s/.\{73,\}/&\n/; ta' file.txt

Resetting the t flag

The t flag is reset at the start of each new input line and also immediately after a successful t branch. This means you can have multiple t tests in one script and they don't interfere with each other across branches.

Infinite Loops

A :label; ...; b label without a guard condition will loop forever. Always ensure the loop has an exit condition — typically the t/T mechanism (which stops branching when the substitution no longer matches), or an address that limits which lines enter the loop.

items.title: Combining sed, awk, and fzf
items.text: The three tools compose naturally in Unix pipelines. Each handles a different layer of the problem.

items.title: The Pattern
items.text:
source cmd  →  awk (shape)  →  sed (clean)  →  fzf (select)  →  awk (extract)  →  action cmd
Stage Role Source find, git, kubectl, grep, cat awk (shape) Extract fields, filter rows, aggregate sed (clean) Strip noise, reformat strings fzf (select) Human picks one or more items awk (extract) Pull the relevant part from the selection Action vim, kubectl, ssh, xargs

You won't always need all six stages, but the principle holds: shape data before the human sees it, extract cleanly after they choose.

items.title: Select a pod to port-forward
items.text:
kubectl get svc -A \
  | awk 'NR>1 {print $1"/"$2, $5}' \
  | sed 's|/TCP||g' \
  | fzf --header "NAMESPACE/NAME  PORTS" \
        --preview 'kubectl describe svc -n $(echo {1} | cut -d/ -f1) $(echo {1} | cut -d/ -f2)' \
  | awk '{print $1}' \
  | while IFS='/' read -r ns svc; do
      read -rp "Local port: " port
      kubectl port-forward -n "$ns" "svc/$svc" "${port}:${port}"
    done

items.title: Find and jump to a TODO
items.text:
grep -rn "TODO\|FIXME" . \
  | sed 's|^\./||' \
  | awk -F: '{print $1, $2, substr($0, index($0,$3))}' \
  | fzf --delimiter=' ' \
        --preview 'bat --color=always --highlight-line {2} {1}' \
        --preview-window='right:60%:+{2}+3/3' \
  | awk '{print "vim +" $2 " " $1}' \
  | bash

items.title: Interactive log error explorer
items.text:
cat app.log \
  | awk '/ERROR|WARN/ { printf "%5d  %-6s  %s\n", NR, $3, substr($0, index($0,$4)) }' \
  | fzf --header "LINE   LEVEL  MESSAGE" \
        --delimiter='  ' \
        --nth=3.. \
        --preview 'awk -v l={1} "NR>=l-5 && NR<=l+10" app.log | bat --color=always' \
        --preview-window=up:40%

items.title: Helm values browser
items.text:
helm show values "$CHART" \
  | awk '
      /^[a-zA-Z]/ { key = $0 }
      /^  [a-zA-Z]/ { print key " > " $0 }' \
  | sed 's/: /=/' \
  | fzf --prompt="Helm value: " \
  | awk -F'=' '{print "--set " $1 "=" $2}'

items.title: Search and open a man page section
items.text:
man -k . \
  | awk '{print $1 "(" $2 ")", $3, $4}' \
  | sed 's/)/):/' \
  | fzf --preview 'man {1} 2>/dev/null | head -40' \
        --preview-window=right:50% \
  | awk '{print $1}' \
  | sed 's/:.*//' \
  | xargs man

items.title: Bulk replace in files selected interactively
items.text:
# Select files to operate on, then sed them in-place
grep -rl "OLD_IMPORT" . \
  | fzf -m --preview 'grep --color=always -n "OLD_IMPORT" {}' \
  | xargs sed -i 's/OLD_IMPORT/NEW_IMPORT/g'

items.title: Generate a kubectl exec command from a fuzzy pod picker
items.text:
# awk extracts NS+POD, fzf selects, awk builds the command
kubectl get pods -A -o wide \
  | awk 'NR>1 { printf "%-20s %-40s %-10s %s\n", $1, $2, $4, $8 }' \
  | fzf --header "NAMESPACE            POD                                       STATUS     NODE" \
        --preview 'kubectl describe pod -n {1} {2}' \
        --preview-window=right:50% \
  | awk '{print "kubectl exec -it -n", $1, $2, "-- /bin/sh"}' \
  | bash

items.title: 1. Shape before displaying
items.text:
Run awk first to extract only the columns fzf needs to show. A narrower display is easier to read and search.

# Bad: fzf sees all 12 kubectl columns
kubectl get pods -A | fzf

# Better: fzf sees only namespace, name, status
kubectl get pods -A | awk 'NR>1 {print $1, $2, $4}' | fzf

items.title: 2. Clean noise before displaying
items.text:
Use sed to strip units, prefixes, or decorators that would make fuzzy matching noisier.

git branch -a \
  | sed 's|remotes/origin/||; s/^[* ]*//' \
  | fzf

items.title: 3. Extract minimally after selection
items.text:
After fzf, use awk to pull out only the field your final command needs. Don't pass the whole line to xargs or bash.

# Bad: passes whole fzf output line
fzf | xargs kubectl delete pod

# Better: extract just the pod name from field 2
fzf | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kubectl delete pod -n "$NS"

items.title: 4. Use --preview to give context
items.text: A preview command that runs kubectl describe, git show, bat, or cat dramatically reduces the number of false selections. The user picks confidently instead of guessing.

items.title: 5. Use --header-lines for table data
items.text:
When your source command already has a header row (like kubectl get pods), pass it through with --header-lines=1 so fzf shows it as a sticky header without making it selectable.

kubectl get pods | fzf --header-lines=1

items.title: sed — Core Commands
items.text: All sed commands beyond substitution. Every command can be prefixed with an address.

items.title: Command Reference
items.text: Cmd Name Description d delete Delete pattern space; start next cycle (no print) p print Print pattern space to stdout P Print Print first line of multi-line pattern space q [N] quit Print pattern space and exit with code N Q [N] Quit Exit without printing (GNU extension) a text append Append text after the current line i text insert Insert text before the current line c text change Replace matched line(s) with text = line number Print current line number to stdout r file read Append contents of file after current line R file Read Read one line from file (GNU) w file write Write pattern space to file W file Write Write first line of pattern space to file (GNU) l list Print pattern space unambiguously (shows , , etc.) y/src/dst/ transliterate Replace characters one-for-one (like tr but per-line) n next Print pattern space, load next line into it N Next Append next line to pattern space with embedded h hold Copy pattern space → hold space H Hold Append pattern space → hold space (with ) g get Copy hold space → pattern space G Get Append hold space → pattern space (with ) x exchange Swap pattern space and hold space : label label Define jump target for branch commands b [label] branch Jump to label (or end of script if no label) t [label] test Branch if any s/// succeeded since last input or t T [label] Test Branch if NO s/// succeeded (GNU); opposite of t e [cmd] execute Execute pattern space as shell command; replace with output (GNU) { ... } block Group multiple commands under one address

items.title: Deleting Lines
items.text:
# Delete blank lines
sed '/^[[:space:]]*$/d' file.txt

# Delete lines containing "TODO"
sed '/TODO/d' source.go

# Delete the first line
sed '1d' file.txt

# Delete the last line
sed '$d' file.txt

# Delete lines 3 through 7
sed '3,7d' file.txt

# Delete lines from /BEGIN/ to EOF
sed '/BEGIN/,$d' file.txt

# Delete lines NOT matching a pattern (keep only matches)
sed '/ERROR/!d' logfile
# Equivalent to: grep ERROR logfile

items.title: Printing Lines
items.text:
# Print only lines 5 to 10 (suppress rest with -n)
sed -n '5,10p' file.txt

# Print only lines matching a pattern
sed -n '/ERROR/p' logfile

# Print first 5 lines then quit
sed '5q' file.txt

# Print line numbers alongside content
sed '=' file.txt | sed 'N;s/\n/\t/'

items.title: Inserting and Appending Text
items.text:
# Insert text BEFORE line 3
sed '3i\--- INSERTED LINE ---' file.txt

# Append text AFTER every line matching /SECTION/
sed '/SECTION/a\    # section ends here' file.txt

# GNU: more readable multiline syntax
sed '/pattern/a\
line one\
line two' file.txt

# Append a blank line after every line (double-space)
sed 'G' file.txt

# Insert a blank line before every line matching /HEADER/
sed '/HEADER/i\\' file.txt

items.title: Changing Lines
items.text:
# Replace lines 3 to 5 entirely with new text
sed '3,5c\[REDACTED]' file.txt

# Replace each line matching /OLD_VERSION/ with a new line
sed '/OLD_VERSION/c\NEW_VERSION: 2.0' file.txt

# Replace a range — the c command applies once to the whole range
sed '/START/,/END/c\[BLOCK REPLACED]' file.txt

items.title: Transliteration
items.text:
# Swap lowercase for uppercase (like tr a-z A-Z)
sed 'y/abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz/ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ/' file

# Replace colons with tabs
sed 'y/:/\t/' file.txt

# ROT13
sed 'y/ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz/NOPQRSTUVWXYZABCDEFGHIJKLMnopqrstuvwxyzabcdefghijklm/' file

y vs s

y does character-for-character replacement across the whole line, like tr. It does not support regex — source and destination must have the same length.

items.title: Read and Write Files
items.text:
# Append the contents of header.txt after line 1
sed '1r header.txt' file.txt

# After each line matching /INJECT/, insert content of snippet.txt
sed '/INJECT/r snippet.txt' template.txt

# Write lines matching ERROR to a separate file
sed -n '/ERROR/w errors.txt' logfile

# Write and still print everything
sed '/ERROR/w errors.txt' logfile

items.title: Quit Early
items.text:
# Print the first 5 lines (faster than head for large files in pipelines)
sed '5q' bigfile.txt

# Print up to (and including) the first line matching a pattern
sed '/FOUND/q' file.txt

# GNU: quit without printing the matching line
sed '/FOUND/Q' file.txt

items.title: Multiple Commands
items.text:
# With -e flags
sed -e 's/foo/bar/' -e 's/baz/qux/' file.txt

# With semicolons (most implementations)
sed 's/foo/bar/;s/baz/qux/' file.txt

# With a block
sed '/ERROR/{
  s/ERROR/CRITICAL/
  s/$/ <<< ALERT/
  w /tmp/critical.log
}' logfile

items.title: sed — In-place Editing
items.text: The -i flag edits files in-place, redirecting output back to the same file. This is one of the most commonly used sed features in scripts and CI pipelines.

items.title: Basic Usage
items.text:
# GNU sed: -i with no suffix (no backup)
sed -i 's/v1\.0/v1.1/g' version.go

# With backup — works on both GNU and BSD sed
sed -i.bak 's/foo/bar/g' config.yaml
# Creates config.yaml.bak before modifying config.yaml

# BSD/macOS sed: -i REQUIRES an argument (even empty string)
sed -i '' 's/foo/bar/g' file.txt   # macOS

items.title: GNU vs BSD Compatibility
items.text:
Behavior GNU sed BSD sed (macOS) -i with no backup sed -i 's/a/b/' Not supported — requires suffix -i with backup sed -i.bak 's/a/b/' sed -i .bak 's/a/b/' (space before suffix) Portable syntax sed -i.bak sed -i.bak

The most portable form is always sed -i.bak — it works on both.

On macOS with GNU sed installed via Homebrew:

# Use gsed to get GNU behavior on macOS
gsed -i 's/foo/bar/g' file.txt

# Or add to PATH: export PATH="$(brew --prefix gnu-sed)/libexec/gnubin:$PATH"

items.title: Editing Multiple Files
items.text:
# Edit multiple files matching a glob
sed -i 's/OLD_API/NEW_API/g' *.go

# Edit all YAML files recursively (GNU find + sed)
find . -name '*.yaml' -exec sed -i 's/image: old/image: new/g' {} +

# With fd (faster)
fd -e yaml -x sed -i 's/image: old/image: new/g' {}

items.title: Scoped In-place Edits
items.text:
# Only replace in specific line range
sed -i '1,5s/Copyright 2023/Copyright 2024/' *.go

# Only edit lines NOT matching a pattern
sed -i '/^#/!s/old/new/g' config.txt

# Only edit the last line
sed -i '$s/old/new/' file.txt

items.title: Common Recipes
items.text:
# Update a version number in a Go file
sed -i 's/version = "[^"]*"/version = "2.0.0"/' cmd/root.go

# Strip trailing whitespace from all Go files
find . -name '*.go' -exec sed -i 's/[[:space:]]*$//' {} +

# Comment out a specific config line
sed -i '/^LoadModule ssl_module/s/^/# /' httpd.conf

# Uncomment a config line
sed -i 's/^# \(LoadModule ssl_module\)/\1/' httpd.conf

# Prepend a shebang line to a script
sed -i '1s/^/#!/usr/bin/env bash\n/' script.sh

# Add a blank line after a specific line
sed -i '/^---$/a\\' doc.md

No Undo

There is no undo for -i without a backup suffix. In any automated script (CI, Makefile), always use -i.bak or test the command thoroughly without -i first. Consider committing the file to git before running bulk replacements.

Test First

Always run the command without -i first to inspect output: 

# Test
sed 's/old/new/g' important.yaml

# Then apply
sed -i 's/old/new/g' important.yaml

items.title: sed — Multi-line Operations
items.text: By default, sed processes one line at a time. The N, P, and D commands let you build a multi-line pattern space — enabling search and replace across line boundaries.

items.title: The N/P/D Triad
items.text: Command Effect N Append the next input line to pattern space, separated by \n P Print only the first line of the (possibly multi-line) pattern space D Delete only the first line of pattern space, then restart the cycle without reading a new line

items.title: Sliding Two-Line Window
items.text:
The canonical N;P;D loop creates a sliding window of two lines, allowing you to match or transform patterns that span a line boundary.

sed 'N; s/foo
bar/REPLACEMENT/; P; D' file.txt

How it works:

  1. N — read current + next line into pattern space (now has between them)
  2. s/foo bar/REPLACEMENT/ — try to match across the newline
  3. P — print only the first line
  4. D — delete the first line, restart cycle (re-execute script with the remaining line)

items.title: Joining Lines
items.text:
# Join every pair of lines with a space
sed 'N;s/\n/ /' file.txt

# Join lines ending with backslash (continuation lines)
sed -E ':a; /\\$/ { N; s/\\\n//; ba }' file.txt

# Join a line ending in comma with the next line
sed -E ':a; /,$/ { N; s/,\n/,/; ba }' file.txt

items.title: Collapsing Blank Lines
items.text:
# Reduce multiple consecutive blank lines to one
sed '/^$/{ N; /^\n$/d }' file.txt

# GNU: more concise
sed -E '/^\s*$/{2,d}' file.txt

# Remove ALL blank lines
sed '/^[[:space:]]*$/d' file.txt

items.title: Deleting / Replacing Blocks
items.text:
# Delete from /BEGIN/ to /END/ (inclusive)
sed '/BEGIN/,/END/d' file.txt

# Replace an entire block with a single line
sed '/BEGIN/,/END/c\[BLOCK REMOVED]' file.txt

# Delete the two lines surrounding a match
sed '/PATTERN/{N;d}' file.txt   # match + next line
sed -n 'h;/PATTERN/!{g;p};h' file.txt  # skip the line before match

items.title: Multi-line Substitution
items.text:
# Remove C-style block comments (single-line only, simplified)
sed 's|/\*[^*]*\*/||g' file.c

# Multi-line C block comment removal (loop)
sed -E ':a; s|/\*[^*]*\*/||g; ta; /\/\*/ { N; ba }' file.c

# Replace a two-line pattern
sed 'N; s/line one\nline two/MERGED/' file.txt

items.title: Print Context Around a Match
items.text:
# Print 2 lines after a match (using N twice)
sed -n '/PATTERN/{p;n;p;n;p}' file.txt

# Print the matched line and the one before it
sed -n '/PATTERN/{x;p;x;p};h' file.txt

The N/P/D Loop Explained

D is the key to making the sliding window work. Unlike d (which starts the next cycle by reading the next input line), D restarts the current cycle with whatever remains in the pattern space — without discarding it and without reading a new line. This means the window slides forward by one line per iteration.

items.title: sed — Practical Recipes
items.text: Real-world one-liners and short scripts organized by category.

items.title: Text Cleaning
items.text:
# Strip ANSI escape codes from terminal output
sed -E 's/\x1B\[[0-9;]*[mK]//g' colored.log

# Normalize Windows CRLF to Unix LF
sed 's/\r//' windows.txt > unix.txt
sed -i 's/\r//' file.txt               # in-place

# Remove trailing whitespace
sed 's/[[:space:]]*$//' file.txt

# Remove leading whitespace
sed 's/^[[:space:]]*//' file.txt

# Remove both leading and trailing whitespace
sed -E 's/^[[:space:]]+|[[:space:]]+$//' file.txt

# Collapse multiple blank lines into one
sed '/./,/^$/!d' file.txt

# Remove all blank lines
sed '/^[[:space:]]*$/d' file.txt

# Remove HTML tags
sed -E 's/<[^>]+>//g' file.html

# Remove XML comments
sed -E 's/<!--[^-]*-->//g; /<!--/,/-->/d' file.xml

items.title: Line Operations
items.text:
# Print only lines 5-10
sed -n '5,10p' file.txt

# Print every other line (odd lines)
sed -n 'p;n' file.txt

# Print every other line (even lines)
sed -n 'n;p' file.txt

# Add line numbers
sed '=' file.txt | sed 'N;s/\n/\t/'

# Reverse line order (tac)
sed -n '1!G;h;$p' file.txt

# Double-space a file
sed 'G' file.txt

# Remove double-spacing
sed -n 'p;n' file.txt

# Print lines between two patterns (inclusive)
sed -n '/^---$/,/^---$/p' document.md

# Print lines between two patterns (exclusive — exclude the delimiters)
sed -n '/^---$/,/^---$/{/^---$/!p}' document.md

# Delete from a matching line to EOF
sed '/^# DO NOT EDIT BELOW/,$d' config.sh

items.title: File Modification
items.text:
# Comment out lines matching a pattern
sed '/^LoadModule ssl/s/^/# /' httpd.conf

# Uncomment lines matching a pattern
sed '/^# LoadModule ssl/s/^# //' httpd.conf

# Insert a line after a matching line
sed '/^kind: Deployment/a\  annotations:' manifest.yaml

# Insert a line before a matching line
sed '/^kind: Deployment/i\# Managed by Flux' manifest.yaml

# Prepend a header to a file (in-place)
sed -i '1s/^/# Generated by build.sh\n/' output.sh

# Replace lines 3 to 5 entirely
sed '3,5c\[REDACTED]' file.txt

# Delete a block from /BEGIN/ to /END/
sed '/BEGIN/,/END/d' file.txt

# Remove duplicate consecutive blank lines
sed '/^$/{ N; /^\n$/d }' file.txt

items.title: Configuration File Surgery
items.text:
# Change a value in a YAML key: value file
sed -i 's/^  replicas: .*/  replicas: 3/' deployment.yaml

# Update a specific Go constant
sed -i 's/const Version = "[^"]*"/const Version = "1.2.3"/' version.go

# Bump semver patch (requires GNU sed /e flag)
sed -E -i 's/(version: "[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.)([0-9]+)(")/echo "\1$((\2+1))\3"/e' Chart.yaml

# Enable a disabled config line (remove leading #)
sed -i '/^#[[:space:]]*ENABLE_FEATURE/s/^#[[:space:]]*//' config

# Replace a URL across all Go files
find . -name '*.go' -exec sed -i 's|https://old.api.example.com|https://new.api.example.com|g' {} +

items.title: Log Processing
items.text:
# Extract lines between timestamps
sed -n '/2024-01-15 09:00/,/2024-01-15 10:00/p' app.log

# Remove log timestamps (ISO format)
sed -E 's/^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9:\.Z+-]+ //' app.log

# Extract just the message part (everything after third |)
sed -E 's/^([^|]*\|){3}//' structured.log

# Redact passwords from logs
sed -E 's/(password|passwd|secret)[=:][^ ]*/\1=REDACTED/gi' app.log

items.title: Code Manipulation
items.text:
# Remove single-line C++ comments
sed 's|//.*||' source.cpp

# Remove Python docstrings (simplified, single line)
sed '/""".*"""/d' script.py

# Add indentation to every line
sed 's/^/    /' code.txt

# Remove indentation (4 spaces)
sed 's/^    //' code.txt

# Wrap long lines at 80 chars
sed ':a; s/.\{81,\}/&\n/; ta' file.txt

items.title: sed as grep
items.text:
# Print only lines matching a pattern (like grep)
sed -n '/ERROR/p' logfile

# Print only non-matching lines (like grep -v)
sed '/ERROR/d' logfile

# Print line number of first match (like grep -n ... | head -1)
sed -n '/PATTERN/=' file.txt | head -1

items.title: sed — Pattern Space & Hold Space
items.text: sed has two internal string buffers. Mastering them unlocks techniques impossible with simple substitution.

items.title: The Two Buffers
items.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PATTERN SPACE                                              │
│  • Holds the current line being processed                   │
│  • Gets cleared at the start of each cycle                  │
│  • Is printed (unless -n) at the end of each cycle          │
│  • Commands like s, d, p operate directly on it             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  HOLD SPACE                                                 │
│  • A scratch buffer that persists across all cycles         │
│  • Starts as a single empty string                          │
│  • Never automatically cleared or printed                   │
│  • Only changed by h, H, g, G, x                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

items.title: Buffer Commands
items.text: Command Effect h Copy pattern space → hold space (overwrites hold) H Append pattern space → hold space (with \n separator) g Copy hold space → pattern space (overwrites pattern) G Append hold space → pattern space (with \n separator) x Swap pattern space and hold space

items.title: Reverse line order (tac equivalent)
items.text:
sed -n '1!G;h;$p' file.txt

Step by step:

Command Meaning 1!G For all lines except line 1, append hold space to pattern space h Copy the (now combined) pattern space back to hold space $p On the last line, print the accumulated hold space

After each cycle, hold space grows: it becomes lineN lineN-1 ... line1, and we print that at the end.

items.title: Print the line BEFORE a match
items.text:
sed -n '/PATTERN/{x;p;x};h' file.txt
  • h saves the current line to hold space every cycle.
  • On a match: x brings the previous line into pattern space, p prints it, x restores.

items.title: Print the line AFTER a match
items.text:
sed -n '/PATTERN/{n;p}' file.txt

n moves to the next line, then p prints it.

items.title: Delete the line after a match
items.text: sed '/PATTERN/{n;d}' file.txt

items.title: Insert a blank line after every line
items.text:
sed 'G' file.txt

Hold space starts empty, so appending it adds a \n (blank line) after each line.

items.title: Remove blank lines added by double-spacing
items.text: sed -n 'p;n' file.txt

items.title: Join every pair of lines
items.text:
sed 'N;s/\n/ /' file.txt

N appends the next line (with \n), then s replaces the newline with a space.

items.title: Swap two adjacent lines
items.text:
sed -n 'h;n;p;g;p' file.txt
# h: save line1 to hold
# n: read line2 into pattern
# p: print line2
# g: get line1 back
# p: print line1

items.title: Accumulate all lines, then process in END-like block
items.text:
# Print line count using hold space
sed -n 'H;${g;s/\n/\n/g;=}' file.txt

For this kind of "process everything at once" work, awk is usually cleaner.

Mental Model

Think of pattern space as a whiteboard that gets erased every cycle, and hold space as a notepad you keep on the desk. h/H/g/G/x are the only ways to move data between them.

items.title: sed — Substitution (s command)
items.text: The s command is the workhorse of sed. It replaces the first (or all) occurrences of a regex in the pattern space.

items.title: Syntax
items.text:
s/REGEX/REPLACEMENT/[FLAGS]

The delimiter / can be any character — use |, #, or @ when the pattern contains slashes.

items.title: Flags
items.text: Flag Effect g Replace all occurrences on the line (not just the first) N (number) Replace only the N-th occurrence p Print the line if substitution was made (useful with -n) i / I Case-insensitive match (GNU sed) e After substitution, execute the pattern space as a shell command (GNU) m / M Multi-line mode: ^ and $ match start/end of each line in pattern space w file Write lines where substitution occurred to file

items.title: Replacement Tokens
items.text: Token Meaning & The entire matched string \1 … \9 Capture group backreference \u Uppercase next character (GNU) \l Lowercase next character (GNU) \U Uppercase everything until \E or end (GNU) \L Lowercase everything until \E or end (GNU) \E End \U or \L transformation (GNU) \n Newline in replacement

items.title: Basic Substitution
items.text:
# Replace first occurrence
sed 's/localhost/127.0.0.1/' nginx.conf

# Replace all occurrences on each line
sed 's/foo/bar/g' file.txt

# Replace only the 2nd occurrence per line
sed 's/foo/bar/2' file.txt

# Replace the 2nd and all subsequent occurrences (GNU: combine N and g)
sed 's/foo/bar/2g' file.txt

# Case-insensitive global replace (GNU)
sed 's/error/ERROR/Ig' logfile

items.title: Using & (whole match)
items.text:
# Wrap every number in brackets
sed 's/[0-9]\+/[&]/g' file.txt

# Quote every word
sed 's/[a-zA-Z]\+/"&"/g' file.txt

# Add parentheses around an IP address
sed 's/[0-9]\{1,3\}\(\.[0-9]\{1,3\}\)\{3\}/(&)/' file.txt

items.title: Capture Groups
items.text:
# Swap first two colon-delimited fields (BRE)
sed 's/\([^:]*\):\([^:]*\)/\2:\1/' /etc/passwd

# Same with ERE (-E) — more readable
sed -E 's/([^:]+):([^:]+)/\2:\1/' /etc/passwd

# Extract just the filename from a path
sed -E 's|.*/([^/]+)$|\1|' paths.txt

# Reformat date from YYYY-MM-DD to DD/MM/YYYY
sed -E 's/([0-9]{4})-([0-9]{2})-([0-9]{2})/\3\/\2\/\1/' dates.txt

items.title: Case Conversion (GNU)
items.text:
# Uppercase the entire line
sed 's/.*/\U&/' file.txt

# Lowercase the entire line
sed 's/.*/\L&/' file.txt

# Title-case: uppercase first letter of each word
sed 's/\b\w/\u&/g' file.txt

# Uppercase only the first word
sed 's/^\w\+/\u&/' file.txt

# Uppercase a specific capture group
sed -E 's/(error|warn)/\U\1\E/gi' logfile

items.title: Alternate Delimiters
items.text:
# Replace paths — avoid escaping slashes
sed 's|/usr/local|/opt|g' Makefile

# Using # as delimiter
sed 's#http://old.host#https://new.host#g' urls.txt

# Using @ as delimiter
sed 's@pattern@replacement@g' file

items.title: Practical Substitutions
items.text:
# Delete trailing whitespace
sed 's/[[:space:]]*$//' file.txt

# Delete leading whitespace
sed 's/^[[:space:]]*//' file.txt

# Remove HTML tags
sed -E 's/<[^>]+>//g' file.html

# Add prefix to every line
sed 's/^/PREFIX: /' file.txt

# Add suffix to every line
sed 's/$/ SUFFIX/' file.txt

# Normalize Windows CRLF to LF
sed 's/\r//' windows.txt

# Collapse multiple spaces into one
sed 's/  */ /g' file.txt

# Remove blank lines
sed '/^[[:space:]]*$/d' file.txt

# Double-quote the third field (colon-delimited)
sed -E 's/^([^:]*:[^:]*:)([^:]*)/\1"\2"/' file

items.title: Print Only Changed Lines
items.text:
# With -n and /p flag: only output lines where substitution occurred
sed -n 's/ERROR/FOUND: &/p' logfile

# Useful for extracting matching lines with transformation
sed -n 's/.*version: "\([^"]*\)".*/\1/p' Chart.yaml

items.title: Execute Replacement as Shell Command (GNU)
items.text:
# /e flag: execute the resulting pattern space as a shell command
# Bump semver patch number using shell arithmetic
sed -E 's/(version: "[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.)([0-9]+)(")/echo "\1$((\2+1))\3"/e' Chart.yaml

# Resolve a symlink inline
sed 's/.*/readlink -f &/e' symlinks.txt

Security

The /e flag passes the pattern space to the shell. Never use it on untrusted input.

items.title: When to Use Which
items.text: A quick decision table for picking the right tool before you start typing.

items.title: Decision Table
items.text: Task Best Tool Why Replace a string in a file sed s/old/new/ is exactly its job Delete lines matching a pattern sed /pattern/d is direct and fast Insert a line before/after a match sed i\ and a\ commands Extract a specific column from CSV/TSV awk $N field splitting is native Sum / count values from a log awk Has variables and arithmetic Multiple transformations per line awk Full control flow; sed gets unwieldy Reformat / print with custom layout awk printf gives full format control Interactively select a file to edit fzf Real-time fuzzy filter over any list Search command history fzf Shell integration replaces Ctrl-R Pick a git branch to checkout fzf Can preview commit graph per selection Complex multi-file transformations Python/Perl Reach for a real language at this point

items.title: When sed Gets Awkward
items.text:
Reach for awk instead of sed when you need:

  • Arithmetic (sed has none)
  • More than one field from a line (sed has no field concept)
  • Counters or aggregations across lines
  • Arrays or associative data structures
  • Multiple conditions with different actions per condition

items.title: When awk Gets Awkward
items.text:
Reach for sed instead of awk when:

  • You just need a simple substitution — sed 's/a/b/' beats an awk program for legibility
  • You need to edit a file in-place with a backup (sed -i.bak)
  • You're doing hold-space tricks like reversing line order or inserting a line relative to another

items.title: When to Add fzf
items.text: Add fzf to any pipeline when a human needs to make a choice from a list. If the selection can be determined programmatically, skip it. If you'd otherwise have to print a numbered list and prompt for input, replace that entire UX with fzf.

items.title: The Composition Sweet Spot
items.text:
# awk extracts → sed cleans → fzf selects → awk extracts → command acts
kubectl get pods -A -o wide \
  | awk 'NR>1 {print $1, $2, $4}' \
  | sed 's/Running/✓/' \
  | fzf --header "NAMESPACE POD STATUS" \
  | awk '{print $1, $2}' \
  | xargs -n2 kubectl logs -f -n

items.title: Gitnexus tips
items.text:
TODO: teach me gitnexus.

CXXFLAGS="-std=c++20" npm install -g gitnexus

npx gitnexus analyze --embeddings --skills -v

items.title: Go — A Field Guide
items.text:
From zero to idiomatic, with all the sharp edges mapped.

Go is a compiled, statically typed language built for simplicity, concurrency, and reliability at scale. This guide covers the language end-to-end — with a focus on why things work the way they do.

items.title: What's covered
items.text:
• Foundations

    Variables, types, functions, closures, structs, interfaces, errors, defer, slices and maps.

    Start here →

  • Gotchas

    The nil interface trap, loop-variable capture, slice sharing, defer in loops, mutex copies, and more.

    See gotchas →

  • Go Proverbs

    Rob Pike's design principles, each with a concrete code example and reasoning.

    Read proverbs →

  • Channel Patterns

    Pipelines, fan-out/fan-in, worker pools, semaphores, done channels, and errgroup.

    Explore patterns →

  • Generics

    Type parameters, constraints, ~ underlying types, and when not to use generics.

    Learn generics →

  • unsafe

    The six legal unsafe.Pointer patterns, the uintptr trap, and zero-copy string/byte tricks.

    Use with care →

items.title: Quick reference
